subject: ap-government
title: SCOTUS Comparison — McCutcheon v. Federal Election Commission (2013)
stimulus:
Alabama businessman Shaun McCutcheon contributed $33,088 to sixteen different candidates running for federal office in 2012. His donations exceeded the aggregate (total) campaign finance limit established by the Bipartisan Campaign Reform Act (BCRA) of 2002. This law limited the total amount of political contributions that an individual could make in a given two-year election period to federal candidates, federal political action committees, and political parties.

In the subsequent case, McCutcheon v. Federal Election Commission (2013), the Court ruled in a 5–4 decision that limits on the amount of political contributions established by the BCRA are unconstitutional. The Court held that "contributing money to a candidate is an exercise of an individual's right to participate in the electoral process. . . . A restriction on how many candidates and committees an individual may support is hardly a 'modest restraint' on those rights. The government may no more restrict how many candidates or causes a donor may support than it may tell a newspaper how many candidates it may endorse."

parts.prompt: Identify the civil liberty that is common in both Citizens United v. Federal Election Commission (2010) and McCutcheon v. Federal Election Commission (2013).
parts.scoring_notes: Only freedom of speech (or First Amendment free speech) earns the point. Accept related phrasings such as 'free speech' or 'First Amendment speech rights.'

scoring_points.description: Identify the civil liberty that is common in both Citizens United v. Federal Election Commission (2010) and McCutcheon v. Federal Election Commission (2013).

required_elements: Identifies freedom of speech (or First Amendment free speech) as the civil liberty common to both cases
alternatives.correct_example: Freedom of speech is the civil liberty that is common to both Citizens United and McCutcheon v. Federal Election Commission.

wrong_examples: Freedom of religion — neither case involved religious expression or practice.
The right to vote — both cases involved campaign contributions and spending, which the Court treated as speech, not the right to vote itself.
common_traps: Accept 'political speech' or 'First Amendment rights' if clearly referring to speech
The Court treated political spending as a form of First Amendment speech in both cases
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for identifying the civil liberty that is common in both Citizens United v. Federal Election Commission (2010) and McCutcheon v. Federal Election Commission (2013). Acceptable response: Freedom of speech.

sample_responses.response: Freedom of speech is the civil liberty that is common to both Citizens United and McCutcheon v. Federal Election Commission.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies freedom of speech as the shared civil liberty in both cases. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: The right to bear arms is common to both cases.
sample_responses.commentary: Incorrect civil liberty — neither case involved the Second Amendment. Both cases centered on the First Amendment's free speech protections as applied to campaign finance. 0 pts.

parts.prompt: Explain how the decision in Citizens United v. Federal Election Commission relates to the reasoning in McCutcheon v. Federal Election Commission.
parts.scoring_notes: First point requires accurate information about Citizens United (the required case). Second point requires connecting the Citizens United decision to the McCutcheon reasoning — both cases applied the First Amendment to strike down campaign finance restrictions, treating political spending/contribution as protected speech.

scoring_points.description: Explain how the Citizens United decision relates to McCutcheon reasoning. (1 point — describe relevant fact or holding about the required Supreme Court case Citizens United v. FEC)

required_elements: Accurately describes the holding or reasoning from Citizens United v. FEC, the required case
alternatives.correct_example: In Citizens United, the Supreme Court held that the First Amendment protects independent political spending by corporations and other organizations, striking down federal limits on independent campaign expenditures.

required_elements: Accurately describes the holding or reasoning from Citizens United v. FEC, the required case
alternatives.correct_example: The Citizens United decision ruled that political advertising by corporations and associations is a form of free speech protected by the First Amendment, and that Congress cannot limit independent expenditures in elections.

wrong_examples: Describes only McCutcheon without mentioning Citizens United — earns 0 points.
States that Citizens United was about voter registration — confusing the case with a different issue.
common_traps: Only Citizens United information is needed for the first point
Both the reasoning and the holding earn the point
scoring_points.official_rubric: The first point is earned for describing a relevant fact or holding from the required case, Citizens United v. FEC. Acceptable response: The Supreme Court in the Citizens United decision ruled that the First Amendment includes protections for independent spending in political campaigns as free speech.

sample_responses.response: The Supreme Court in the Citizens United decision ruled that the First Amendment includes protections for independent spending in political campaigns as free speech.
sample_responses.commentary: Accurately describes the holding from Citizens United — First Amendment protects independent campaign spending as free speech. Earns the first point.

sample_responses.response: In McCutcheon v. FEC, the Supreme Court struck down aggregate contribution limits as unconstitutional under the First Amendment.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes only McCutcheon without providing any information about Citizens United, the required case. 0 pts for this point.

scoring_points.description: Explain how the Citizens United decision relates to McCutcheon reasoning. (2 points — correctly explains how reasoning in BOTH cases is connected)

required_elements: Explains the common reasoning thread connecting both cases — both applied First Amendment speech protections to strike down campaign finance restrictions
alternatives.correct_example: In Citizens United, the Court reasoned that independent corporate political spending is protected speech and Congress cannot limit it. McCutcheon extended that reasoning — the Court held that aggregate contribution limits also violated the First Amendment because contributing to political campaigns is a form of protected political speech. Both cases apply the same principle: government restrictions on political spending/contributions face First Amendment scrutiny, and broad limits are unconstitutional.

wrong_examples: Describes only one case — earns 1 point (b1) only.
States both cases are about 'campaign finance' without explaining how the Citizens United reasoning influenced McCutcheon.
common_traps: Must address BOTH cases to earn the second point
The key connection is that McCutcheon built on Citizens United's reasoning that campaign finance restrictions must satisfy strict First Amendment scrutiny
scoring_points.official_rubric: The second point is earned for correctly explaining how the reasoning in both cases is connected. Acceptable response: These were both cases that considered whether campaign finance laws passed by Congress were constitutional. In both cases the Supreme Court held that spending limitations were in violation of the free speech clause of the First Amendment.

sample_responses.response: These were both cases that considered whether campaign finance laws passed by Congress were constitutional. In both cases the Supreme Court held that spending limitations were in violation of the free speech clause of the First Amendment.
sample_responses.commentary: Addresses both cases and explains the common constitutional thread — both Courts applied the First Amendment's free speech clause to strike down campaign finance restrictions. Earns the second point.

sample_responses.response: Both cases involved campaign finance and the Court made similar decisions.
sample_responses.commentary: Too vague — does not explain the specific First Amendment reasoning that connects Citizens United to McCutcheon. Naming campaign finance without articulating the shared constitutional logic does not earn the point. 0 pts.

parts.prompt: Explain how the decision in McCutcheon v. Federal Election Commission can be used to support the participatory, pluralist, or elite model of democracy.
parts.scoring_notes: Accept any of the three models as long as the response correctly explains the connection between the McCutcheon decision and the chosen model. The response must explain how the ruling supports the model, not just name the model.

scoring_points.description: Explain how the decision in McCutcheon v. FEC can be used to support the participatory, pluralist, or elite model of democracy.

required_elements: Identifies one of the three models and explains a specific connection between the McCutcheon ruling and that model
alternatives.correct_example: The McCutcheon decision supports the elite model of democracy because it removes aggregate limits on political contributions, allowing wealthy individuals to donate to many more candidates and exert disproportionate influence over elections — concentrating political power among economic elites who can afford to make large donations.

required_elements: Identifies one of the three models and explains a specific connection
alternatives.correct_example: The decision supports the pluralist model because it allows interest groups and organized groups of like-minded individuals to pool their political contributions, giving competing groups more freedom to participate financially in the political process and influence outcomes.

required_elements: Identifies one of the three models and explains a specific connection
alternatives.correct_example: The decision could support the participatory model because it expands an individual's right to financially participate in elections by contributing to more candidates, broadening the forms of democratic participation available to individual citizens.

wrong_examples: Simply naming a model without explaining how McCutcheon supports it.
Describing the decision's outcome without connecting it to any model of democracy.
common_traps: Any of the three models can earn the point — the key is a specific and accurate explanation of how the ruling supports the chosen model
Responses that accurately connect the ruling to a model earn the point even if their model choice could be disputed
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for explaining how the decision in McCutcheon v. FEC can be used to support the participatory, pluralist, or elite model of democracy. Acceptable responses include: Elite model — limits on contributions only affects those with the money to contribute more, giving wealthy elites more influence; Pluralist model — allows groups to combine their political power through financial contributions; Participatory model — individuals can contribute financially to candidates as a form of participation in the democratic process.

sample_responses.response: The McCutcheon decision supports the elite model of democracy because by removing aggregate limits on political contributions, the wealthy are able to have more influence over elections than the average citizen.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies the elite model and explains how removing contribution limits concentrates political influence among wealthy donors — a direct connection to the elite model. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: The decision supports the participatory model because it allows individuals to contribute to more candidates, expanding the ways citizens can participate in democracy.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies the participatory model and explains how the ruling expands individual financial participation in elections. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: The McCutcheon decision is about campaign finance and democracy.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not identify a model of democracy or explain how the ruling supports any specific model. 0 pts.
